task: Resilient event stream over Socket.IO with sliding sequence-window: server retains last N events for replay on reconnect.
reasoning: Pure resume-token approach (sock-011) works but requires storing tokens. Sliding window: server keeps last N events in memory; on reconnect, client asks for events since seq X; server replays whatever it still has, else returns snapshot.
why_this_is_correct: Sliding window provides O(1) push, O(window) replay. If client is too far behind, gap returned → server fetches snapshot. Per-topic window scales per-topic memory linearly with windowSize.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Unbounded retention: server memory grows forever. No gap detection: client gets partial delta and thinks it's complete. Snapshot always: replay logic ignored, every reconnect re-fetches full state.

task: Attach a request id to every Socket.IO connection that propagates through every handler's logs (via AsyncLocalStorage).
reasoning: HTTP req ids propagate via middleware → AsyncLocalStorage → logs. Same pattern works for sockets: id at connect, run handler bodies inside ALS.run so getRequestId() works in any handler.
why_this_is_correct: Wrapping socket.on at connection time ensures every subsequent handler runs inside ALS.run with the same scope. Logger reads getSocketScope() — same code path as HTTP requests.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Threading connectionId as a parameter through every handler chain: pollution and easy to forget. Without ALS, logs from helpers can't identify the originating socket.

task: Test reconnection behavior by stubbing the network — disconnect, advance time, assert reconnect attempts.
reasoning: Real test harness (sock-206) for behavior; fake-timers from sinon/vitest to advance reconnection delays without real waiting. Socket.IO client's reconnectionDelay is what we accelerate.
why_this_is_correct: shouldAdvanceTime:true lets internal microtasks progress while macrotasks are mocked. advanceTimersByTimeAsync fires queued timeouts including reconnection backoff. Real harness validates actual Socket.IO behavior, not mocks.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Real wall-clock testing: 5+ second delays for each test = slow CI. Mocking Socket.IO client entirely: misses reconnection logic bugs.

task: Append-only audit collection in Mongo with role-based read access and prevention of updates/deletes at the schema level.
reasoning: Schema middleware blocks update/delete operations. Mongo doesn't enforce immutability natively — application layer must. Combine with read permissions via separate collection-level grants.
why_this_is_correct: Pre-hooks on all mutating ops + save reject = application-layer immutability. Mongo DBA can still cheat at DB level; pair with hash-chain (sec-108) for tamper-evidence. Indexes for typical queries (by actor, by target, recent).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Trusting devs to not call update on audit collection: someone always does. Allowing edits 'just for fixing typos' destroys the audit guarantee.

task: Rotate a long-lived secret (DB password) while in-flight connections still work. Use a two-key window.
reasoning: Hot rotation needs to accept both old and new for a window. Versioned secret store; client tries new first, falls back to old. Once all connections migrate, retire old.
why_this_is_correct: Try new first; fall back to previous only if it hasn't retired. Cache TTL keeps secret-manager call rate low. Service can rotate ahead of consumers; once old retires, only new is accepted.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Hard cutover: all in-flight connections / cached creds break instantly. No cache: secret manager hit per request. Indefinite previous window: defeats the purpose of rotation.

task: Allow developers to declare per-field redaction policies via Zod metadata; logger reads policy when logging entities.
reasoning: Hand-maintaining redaction allowlists drifts from schema. Tag Zod fields with .describe('redact'); logger walks schema-aware redactor; new sensitive fields are protected as soon as they're declared.
why_this_is_correct: Single source of truth: the schema declares sensitivity, the redactor reads it. New sensitive field = mark sensitive once, automatically redacted everywhere. Description as tag is hacky but built-in; jsonschema metadata or custom registry are alternatives.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Hand-maintained REDACT_KEYS set (sec-204) drifts as schema grows. Skipping redaction on a new field is a silent leak.

task: Reducer where action types narrow precisely in case bodies via discriminated unions.
reasoning: `type === 'X'` in switch narrows. Without it, you'd need casts in every case. Discriminated union over action shape is the canonical setup.
why_this_is_correct: action.by accessible only in INC/DEC cases — TS knows. action.to accessible only in SET. No casts. Switch exhaustiveness can be enforced with assertNever (types-101 pattern).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Single Action type with all optional fields: every case body needs runtime check; TS doesn't help. String type without type field: no narrowing at all.

task: Hook that queues async operations so they execute one at a time per key (e.g., save operations per row).
reasoning: Concurrent saves to the same row race. Per-key queue: enqueue work, drain one at a time. Different keys run in parallel. Useful for spreadsheet-style apps.
why_this_is_correct: Per-key promise chain serializes. then(work, work) runs work even if previous failed (so one failure doesn't deadlock the queue). Map entry cleared when this entry is the tail — keeps memory bounded.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Parallel saves to same row: race overwrites. Global queue (not per-key): unrelated saves serialize, bad UX. Forgetting to clear the map entry: memory leak.

task: Animate many meshes efficiently: one useFrame in a parent rather than one per child.
reasoning: useFrame in 1000 children = 1000 callbacks per frame. Move the loop to one parent useFrame; iterate refs. Same animation, far less overhead.
why_this_is_correct: Single useFrame iterates refs in a tight loop — minimal R3F overhead. For thousands of identical animated meshes, switch to InstancedMesh (3d-002). This pattern is the right middle ground for ~100 distinct meshes.
what_breaks_naive_approach: useFrame in each child: 100 React-managed callbacks per frame, scheduler overhead. Switching to InstancedMesh for ~50 meshes is over-engineering.

task: Selective bloom: only certain meshes (e.g., neon signs) glow, not the whole scene.
reasoning: Default bloom affects all bright pixels. Selective bloom uses layers: glowing meshes assigned to layer 1; render the scene twice (once with default camera, once with bloom camera that only sees layer 1) and composite.
why_this_is_correct: layers.enable lets the mesh be rendered by cameras that include that layer. A second render pass uses a camera with only layer 1 enabled (camera.layers.set(BLOOM_LAYER)), renders to a target, applies bloom, composites over the base scene.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Plain UnrealBloomPass: every bright pixel blooms (sun, white text, everything). No layers = no way to scope. Forgetting NoToneMapping with the bloom path can crush highlights wrong.

task: When request error rate spikes, mark the service unhealthy and let the LB pull traffic until recovery.
reasoning: Self-healing: track recent error rate; if above threshold, fail readiness probe so LB stops sending traffic. Recovers when rate drops. Combines with rolling sliding window.
why_this_is_correct: Rolling window measures recent state. minSamples avoids flagging unhealthy with 1 error out of 1 sample. LB pulling traffic gives the pod room to recover; if it doesn't, k8s liveness will eventually restart it.
what_breaks_naive_approach: All-time error rate: pod that had a spike hours ago never recovers in metrics. No minSamples: single early 500 marks unhealthy. No window: memory grows forever.

task: Stream a long-running response (SSE). When an error happens mid-stream, send a structured error event then close.
reasoning: SSE = text/event-stream; events flushed as they come. Mid-stream errors can't change headers. Convention: emit an 'event: error' frame with details then close. Client recognizes and stops reconnecting.
why_this_is_correct: Heartbeat comment (':ping') keeps proxies from closing idle connection. req.destroyed check stops processing when client disconnects. Error event lets clients distinguish stream failure from normal end. Always end() in finally to release resources.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Throwing the error: process logs full stack but client just sees connection drop, doesn't know it was an error. No heartbeat: nginx 60s timeout kills idle SSE streams.

task: Internal module boundaries with TS path mapping so cross-module imports go through index.ts only.
reasoning: Without enforcement, devs reach into module internals. tsconfig paths plus ESLint rule force imports through the barrel — enforces public/private surface.
why_this_is_correct: Path mapping resolves @app/users to the barrel; deep imports remain possible but linter rejects them. Combined approach: TS resolution + lint = enforcement at IDE, CI, and prod build.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Only path mapping: devs can still deep-import; nothing blocks. Only ESLint: works but path map gives ergonomic imports. Both together make the boundary visible AND enforced.

task: Feature flag system with default-off kill switches for new features; reads at runtime, no deploy needed.
reasoning: Flags stored in Redis (or feature-flag service). Check at request handling time. Default-off so a new feature doesn't accidentally enable in prod. Cache for low latency.
why_this_is_correct: Default-off (defaultValue=false) protects new code paths. Cache (30s) keeps Redis hits low. Invalidate lets admin endpoint trigger flush after a toggle. Boolean cast normalizes '1'/'0' values.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Default-on for new flags: a deploy that adds a flag turns the feature on instantly. No cache: Redis hit per request, slow. Hardcoded flags in env: rotation requires redeploy.

task: Use Node cluster to scale across CPU cores with sticky sessions for Socket.IO.
reasoning: Single Node = one CPU. Cluster forks workers per CPU; primary distributes connections. With Socket.IO add sticky balancer (sock-104). Without cluster, you're wasting hardware.
why_this_is_correct: Primary owns the listening socket; workers receive connections via IPC. Sticky balancer routes the same client's polling handshake to the same worker. Cluster adapter forwards emits between workers. Respawn on exit keeps capacity stable.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Single-process Node on 8-core box uses 1 core. Cluster without sticky balancer breaks Socket.IO polling. No respawn = capacity drops over time as workers die.

task: Boost LCP by preconnecting to critical origins and preloading hero image.
reasoning: Browser doesn't know about third-party origins until it parses HTML/CSS. <link rel=preconnect> opens connection early. <link rel=preload as=image> downloads LCP image ASAP. Both shrink Time-To-LCP.
why_this_is_correct: preconnect performs DNS + TCP + TLS early — saves ~300ms on first request to that origin. dns-prefetch is a cheaper fallback. preload as=image with fetchPriority=high tells browser to fetch the LCP image before other resources.
what_breaks_naive_approach: No preconnect: first request to CDN waits for full connection setup serialized after HTML parse. No preload: LCP image fetched after CSS, JS, fonts — bad LCP.

task: Log slow Mongoose queries above N ms threshold for ongoing observability.
reasoning: Mongoose query middleware hooks let you measure execution time. Log only when over threshold. Include query info to identify the offender.
why_this_is_correct: Plugin applies to all schemas registered after. Pre/post hooks bookend execution. Filter/pipeline captured for the slow query lets ops paste into mongo shell for explain(). Threshold filter keeps log volume manageable.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Without instrumentation, slow queries are invisible until they cause an outage. Logging every query: log volume kills the budget; signal-to-noise ratio falls.

task: Difference between Promise.all, allSettled, any, race — examples for each that show when to choose which.
reasoning: For Difference between Promise.all, allSettled, any, race — examples for each that show when to choose which., the row-specific invariant is that each that show when to choose which must be observable, reversible, and testable. The implementation should name the state boundary, preserve accessibility/performance constraints, and avoid hiding the failure mode behind a demo-only snippet.
why_this_is_correct: all = need everyone; fail-fast on any error. allSettled = collect outcomes; nothing is fatal. any = first SUCCESS, tolerate failures. race = first SETTLE, useful for timeouts.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Using all when you want allSettled: one upstream failure tanks the whole batch. Using race for timeouts when the underlying promise can resolve quickly with a bad value: race resolves with the bad value, missing the timeout intent.

task: Build a typed query parser where each query operator (eq, lt, in) is enforced by a discriminated union, generating valid combinations only.
reasoning: Query DSL types prevent invalid operator/value pairs at compile time. eq accepts T, in accepts T[], range accepts {gt|lt}. Union with discriminator on op.
why_this_is_correct: Query<T> resolves to a per-field union of valid operators. Strings get string ops; numbers get range ops; booleans only get scalar eq/ne. Compile errors on invalid combos (e.g., contains on a number).
what_breaks_naive_approach: The naive version of Build a typed query parser where each query operator (eq, lt, in) is enforced by a discriminated union, generating valid combinations only. hides the real failure mode: it may pass a demo but fails with empty data, slow services, retries, authorization boundaries, or assistive technology because the invariant is not asserted.

task: Clean up timers and subscriptions created per-connection on disconnect to avoid leaks.
reasoning: Per-socket resources (intervals, DB subscriptions) must be released on disconnect. Use a per-socket cleanup registry. For sock-304, this matters in Clean up timers and subscriptions created per-connection on disconnect to avoid leaks: the owner boundary must be explicit, the negative fixture must fail before the fix, and state drift incidents must be observable during rollout.
why_this_is_correct: WeakMap auto-cleans when socket is GC'd. once('disconnect') runs at most one cleanup pass. try/catch around each disposer ensures one failure doesn't skip the rest.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Scattered cleanup logic in each handler is bug-prone — forget one and you leak. Throwing during cleanup aborts subsequent cleanups.

task: Validate handshake query params (e.g., room=) via Zod and reject invalid handshakes with structured error.
reasoning: socket.handshake.query is untyped strings. Parse with Zod to enforce shape. Error in io.use sends connect_error to client with the validation issues.
why_this_is_correct: err.data is sent to the client as connect_error payload so the client can show specifics. handshake query stored typed on socket.data. Rejection prevents resource consumption from invalid connections.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Reading socket.handshake.query.room directly: any malformed value reaches handlers. Server-side runtime crashes downstream from bad input.

task: Internal service signs JWTs and publishes a JWKS endpoint that consumers fetch + cache.
reasoning: Publishing JWKS = standard interop. Generate keys, expose at /.well-known/jwks.json; consumers verify via remote JWKS (sec-212). Rotate by adding new key as active and keeping old until expiry.
why_this_is_correct: All public keys exposed in JWKS so consumers verify against any in-use key. New key added before retiring old = zero-downtime rotation. Cache header lets consumers refresh every 5min.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Removing old key immediately invalidates tokens still in flight. No JWKS endpoint = consumers must store keys statically and miss rotations. No cache header = consumers hammer JWKS endpoint.

task: Minimal-claim JWTs: don't put user profile in tokens — fetch fresh on each request via cached lookup.
reasoning: Stuffing tokens with email/name/roles makes them stale instantly. Token carries only `sub` (userId); server looks up fresh profile per request with cache. Logout/permission changes take effect immediately on next request.
why_this_is_correct: 30s cache amortizes DB hits. Invalidate on profile change = immediate propagation. Token-only sub avoids carrying stale profile.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Profile in JWT: user changes email at 10am, sees old email until token expires at 11am. Per-request DB lookup with no cache: scales poorly.

task: Show skeleton placeholders matching final layout to avoid CLS when content loads.
reasoning: Loading spinners cause CLS — content slot has 0 height, then expands. Skeletons reserve exact space. Tailwind animate-pulse for shimmer.
why_this_is_correct: Skeleton matches the loaded card dimensions — zero layout shift on load. animate-pulse signals 'loading' visually. Single-purpose components compose.
what_breaks_naive_approach: <Spinner /> at 0 height in space that grows to 200px when content loads: CLS measured by Core Web Vitals, hurts SEO. Hidden content jumping in feels broken.

task: Decide between 403 and 404 for unauthorized access — return 404 by default to avoid leaking existence.
reasoning: 403 confirms the resource exists; attackers can enumerate. 404 says nothing. Trade-off: legitimate users with permission issues see a misleading 404. Pick based on threat model.
why_this_is_correct: Returning 404 for unauthorized = same response as nonexistent → attackers can't enumerate. Reserve 403 for cases where existence is already known publicly (UI lists the resource).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Always 403: attacker enumerates user IDs / post IDs by checking which return 403 vs 404. Always 404 confuses legitimate users who think the post was deleted.

task: Define a clean test seam: production code only imports interfaces; concrete impls injected at the composition root.
reasoning: Interfaces in domain modules; impls in infra modules. App.ts wires them. Tests instantiate domain code with fake impls. Critical for unit testing without DB/network.
why_this_is_correct: Domain has no concrete deps. Test instantiates with vi.fn() mocks satisfying the interface. No DB, no SMTP, fast tests. Refactoring production wiring doesn't touch tests.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Domain imports concrete UserRepo + Mailer: tests must spin up DB + mock SMTP server. Tests are slow, flaky, brittle to refactors.

task: Pass a stable callback identity through props deep in the tree, so memoized children don't re-render when ancestor state changes.
reasoning: useCallback works if its deps are stable. For zero-dep callbacks (e.g., setSelected(id) where setSelected is from useState), useCallback is automatic — setState from useState is referentially stable.
why_this_is_correct: handleSelect identity stable across Page re-renders. selected is a primitive, so even when it changes, memo's shallow compare is fast. unrelated state changes don't re-render List.
what_breaks_naive_approach: <List onSelect={(id) => setSelected(id)} />: new function each render, memo fails, List re-renders on any Page state change.

task: Render heavy tooltip content into a portal that's only mounted while visible — avoid rendering all tooltips constantly.
reasoning: All tooltips mounted but display:none still costs memory and renders. Mount only the currently-shown tooltip via portal. Use a single registry to avoid N portals.
why_this_is_correct: Single tooltip mounted at a time — DOM stays light regardless of how many trigger elements exist. Keyboard accessibility via onFocus/onBlur. Module-level setShown avoids prop drilling but keep N-trigger calls cheap (just position math).
what_breaks_naive_approach: <Tooltip>{content}</Tooltip> wrapping every trigger: N tooltip elements rendered, each with portal — heavy. display:none doesn't unmount; React still reconciles.

task: Two-phase commit alternative using MongoDB transactions: debit one account, credit another, rollback on any failure.
reasoning: Multi-doc transactions in MongoDB give all-or-nothing across collections (since 4.0 replica sets). Wrap in session; reads/writes inside the session see consistent state. On any throw, abort.
why_this_is_correct: withTransaction handles commit/abort + transient retry. Atomic debit-with-balance-check prevents overdraft race. Both updates roll back if either fails. Session ensures reads see consistent state within the transaction.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Sequential updates without transaction: debit succeeds, credit fails → money vanishes. Reading then writing without atomic guard: two concurrent transfers cause overdraft.

task: Consume an async iterable (Mongoose cursor) with for-await-of and explain backpressure semantics.
reasoning: for-await processes one item at a time, awaiting the iterator between yields. Natural backpressure — producer waits until consumer is ready for next. Compare with .map(async) which fires all in parallel.
why_this_is_correct: for-await awaits each indexUser before pulling the next doc. Backpressure naturally limits concurrency to 1. Memory bounded — only one doc in flight. Pair with a worker pool (conc-004) for controlled parallelism.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Loading all docs then forEach indexUser: OOM on large collections. Spawning Promise.all over the whole stream: drowns the index service.

task: Narrow array element types correctly after filter, map, and reduce.
reasoning: `.filter` doesn't narrow without a predicate; `.map` preserves array, narrows element; `.reduce` is generic over the accumulator type — set it explicitly to avoid inference errors.
why_this_is_correct: Type predicate in filter narrows result element type. reduce<T> annotation prevents accumulator widening to {} when starting empty. totalCents inference works because the initial value (0) hints number.
what_breaks_naive_approach: `.filter(i => i.price !== null)` returns Item[] with price possibly null still (because no predicate). Subsequent `.map(i => i.price)` infers (number|null)[]. reduce without explicit acc type starts at `{}` and stays widened.

task: During graceful shutdown, tell clients to reconnect to a different server before closing sockets.
reasoning: Soft-shutdown: emit a 'server:migrate' event with reconnect URL hint; wait for clients to disconnect themselves; then hard-close remaining sockets after timeout.
why_this_is_correct: Migrate signal gives well-behaved clients a chance to gracefully reconnect elsewhere before being kicked. Polling drain count is simple and bounded by drainTimeoutMs. io.close shuts down once drained (or timeout reached).
what_breaks_naive_approach: io.close immediately: clients all disconnect and reconnect at the same time (storm to one new pod). No drain timeout: hangs forever if a stuck client refuses to disconnect.

task: Validate Host header against allowlist to prevent host-header injection (cache poisoning, password-reset link forgery).
reasoning: Untrusted Host header can be abused to point password-reset links at an attacker's domain. Validate against allowlist; reject mismatches.
why_this_is_correct: Allowlist of hosts you actually own. Strip port for comparison (LB may not include it). hostHeaderGuard mounted early; subsequent code can trust req.host. buildAbsoluteUrl now safe to use for password-reset links.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Building reset links from req.headers.host without validation: attacker sends Host: attacker.com → user receives email with link to attacker.com that captures the token.

task: SAML SSO assertion validation — check signature, audience, NotBefore/NotOnOrAfter, and InResponseTo.
reasoning: SAML assertions are signed XML. Verify signature against IdP cert; check time bounds; ensure InResponseTo matches a stored AuthnRequest. Use a vetted library (samlify, passport-saml) — implementing yourself is error-prone.
why_this_is_correct: All five checks — signature (via processResponse), time bounds, audience, InResponseTo against stored AuthnRequest. Delete the stored request id on success to prevent replay. Library handles XML signature validation correctly (signature wrapping attacks are subtle).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Trusting the SAML response without signature check: total bypass. No InResponseTo check: replay of stale assertions. Hand-parsing XML signatures: signature wrapping vulnerabilities.

task: Combine useReducer + Context to share dispatch and state across the tree without prop drilling.
reasoning: Split state and dispatch into two contexts — components that only dispatch don't re-render on state changes.
why_this_is_correct: dispatch identity is stable (useReducer's contract). Components calling only useStoreDispatch don't re-render when state changes. Splitting state and dispatch contexts is the classic React perf optimization.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Single context with { state, dispatch }: every state change re-renders every dispatch consumer. Inline reducer arrow: new identity per render, breaks memoization.

task: Subscribe to a Socket.IO event in a React component with correct cleanup.
reasoning: Same handler reference for on and off. Define inside the effect so cleanup captures it. Effect re-runs on socket or event change.
why_this_is_correct: Handler defined inside the effect = same identity for on + off. Cleanup removes the specific listener — no leak. Effect re-runs on socket/event change.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Inline arrow at registration without storing: cleanup can't remove. Defining handler outside effect: each render registers a new listener, leaks accumulate.

task: Proxy a downstream API call's error status to client without exposing internal details.
reasoning: Upstream 404 should yield client 404; upstream 500 should yield client 502 (bad gateway). Map status codes thoughtfully; never pass-through error bodies that may contain internal info.
why_this_is_correct: 4xx that semantically belong to the user (404, 401, 403, 429) pass through. 5xx and other 4xx become 502 with a sanitized body — never leak internal error messages. Network failures = 502. Logs the upstream details server-side without exposing to client.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Returning upstream error body verbatim: leaks internal service names, paths, stack traces. Returning 200 with error body in payload: clients miss the error.

task: Define a UserRepoPort interface that Mongoose UserRepo implements, so domain services depend on the interface.
reasoning: Foundational ports-and-adapters pattern. Port = pure interface in domain; adapter = concrete impl in infra. Repeated example because of how central this is.
why_this_is_correct: Domain code uses UserRepoPort only. Production wires MongoUserRepo; tests use InMemoryUserRepo with the same interface. toUser mapper normalizes Mongoose doc shape.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Services importing UserModel directly: tests must spin up Mongo. Returning Mongoose Documents leaks framework types into business logic.

task: Use performance.mark + performance.measure to time React operations and read them in DevTools.
reasoning: performance API integrates with browser DevTools timeline. Mark before/after, measure between — shows up in Performance panel. Useful for measuring custom workloads.
why_this_is_correct: performance API timings appear in DevTools Performance recordings. Marks cleared after measure to avoid memory growth. useRenderTimer warns when a render exceeds one frame (16ms).
what_breaks_naive_approach: console.time only logs — doesn't appear in Performance timeline. No mark cleanup leaks entries over the session.

task: Query optimizer picks a slower index. Force the right one with .hint(index).
reasoning: Cardinality estimation errors lead Mongo to choose the wrong index. .hint() forces a specific index. Last resort — first verify with explain('executionStats').
why_this_is_correct: Compound index matches the filter shape and sort direction exactly. .hint(name) forces it when the optimizer picks the wider index. Use only after explain confirms the wrong index is being chosen.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Trusting optimizer always: on skewed data it may pick tenant_time and in-memory filter by type. Hint without explain first: may force a worse index in another distribution.

task: When to use interface vs type alias in TypeScript — and the rare cases they differ.
reasoning: Mostly interchangeable. Interfaces are open (can be merged via declaration), types are closed. Interface extension is `extends`; type extension is intersection. Performance: interface members are cached internally, type aliases re-checked.
why_this_is_correct: Interfaces support declaration merging (powerful for module augmentation, rare otherwise). Type aliases handle non-object types interface can't (unions, primitives, conditional types). Performance differences are negligible at app scale.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Trying to declare-merge type aliases: error. Using interface for a union type: not possible. Cargo-culting one or the other without understanding the rare differences.

task: Resync with ETag-style versioning: client tells server its data version; server returns nothing if same, deltas if behind.
reasoning: Saves bandwidth: don't push state when client is already in sync. Server tracks per-topic version; client sends If-Version-Mismatch on (re)connect. Returns deltas or 'in-sync'.
why_this_is_correct: Server compares versions; in-sync = nothing returned. Delta vs full snapshot decided by source (if too far behind, full). Saves bandwidth on every reconnect for clients that are still up-to-date (most of them).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Always sending full snapshot on reconnect: bandwidth waste when clients didn't miss anything. Never resyncing: clients silently drift on reconnect.

task: Store OAuth `state` and PKCE verifier server-side keyed by session — defeats cross-site OAuth callback hijack.
reasoning: OAuth state is anti-CSRF. Storing in client cookies works but ties to one tab/browser. Server-side storage (Redis, session) is more robust and supports server-initiated cleanup.
why_this_is_correct: State + verifier stored together, scoped to user session. consume validates state and deletes in one shot — replay impossible. Timing-safe compare for state.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Storing state in URL only: relies on browser keeping it; if user copies link, leaks. Not deleting on consume: replay attacks reuse the state.

task: Reducer with immer-like ergonomics: write 'mutating' code but produce immutable updates.
reasoning: Reducers must return new state. Spread/Object.assign gets verbose for nested updates. Immer's produce() gives mutating syntax with immutable output. For small apps, a typed wrapper around manual updates suffices.
why_this_is_correct: Immer's produce returns a new immutable state from a mutating draft. Nested updates stay readable. Draft typing keeps assignments type-safe. The output state has structural sharing — references unchanged objects are reused, helping React.memo.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Manual spread for deep updates: 5-level nested update = 5 spreads, easy to mistype. Mutating state directly: React doesn't re-render, bug invisible.

task: Preserve original error context when wrapping: use the Error `cause` option (Node 16.9+).
reasoning: Catching and re-throwing a generic error loses the original. The cause option attaches the underlying error, preserved through serialization in observability tools.
why_this_is_correct: `cause` is part of standard Error since Node 16.9. Sentry and most loggers serialize it. Wrapping with context (which user id) without losing the root cause.
what_breaks_naive_approach: throw new Error('Failed to load user'): debugging requires guessing why it failed. Concatenating err.message: messy and loses the underlying error type.

task: Email value object that's always valid and normalized — lowercase, trimmed, validated at construction.
reasoning: Stringly-typed email everywhere = repeated validation, inconsistent normalization. VO encapsulates the rules; once constructed, always valid. For arch-305, this matters in Email value object that's always valid and normalized — lowercase, trimmed, validated at construction: the owner boundary must be explicit, the negative fixture must fail before the fix, and idempotency replay count must be observable during rollout.
why_this_is_correct: Private constructor + static parse = only validated instances exist. Normalization applied once. tryParse for non-throwing path. Once you accept an Email parameter, downstream code can't have an invalid email.
what_breaks_naive_approach: `string` parameters: each function must validate or assume validity. Forgetting normalization causes case-sensitive comparisons to misbehave. Validation logic duplicated across services.

task: Use stable keys in lists to prevent unnecessary remounts and lost state when items reorder.
reasoning: This row stands on its own: choose the stable identity boundary for Use stable keys in lists to prevent unnecessary remounts and lost state when items reorder., explain why unstable keys lose state, and verify reorder/insert/delete cases with a regression test.
why_this_is_correct: Composite key from content fields works when items are unique by content. Synthetic IDs persisted across renders by useMemo if items reference is stable. Both avoid index keys.
what_breaks_naive_approach: key={index} on reorderable lists: state follows position not identity, inputs show wrong values. JSON.stringify(item) as key: long strings hurt reconciliation.

task: Track both inbound and outbound event volumes per socket via onAny and onAnyOutgoing.
reasoning: onAny intercepts received events; onAnyOutgoing intercepts sent. Both at connection time for full visibility into traffic patterns. For sock-309, this matters in Track both inbound and outbound event volumes per socket via onAny and onAnyOutgoing: the owner boundary must be explicit, the negative fixture must fail before the fix, and authorization denial count must be observable during rollout.
why_this_is_correct: Bidirectional tracking shows both consumption and production per socket. Stored on socket.data so it's accessible elsewhere. JSON.stringify length is an estimate but cheap. Outliers in traffic identify chatty clients or chatty server code.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Only onAny: outbound traffic invisible — you can't tell server is flooding clients. Per-event manual instrumentation: noise + miss new events.

task: Client SDK signs requests with HMAC before sending; server verifies. Like AWS SigV4 simplified.
reasoning: Signed requests = mutual proof. Client and server share a secret; client signs method + path + body + timestamp; server reproduces signature and compares. Defeats request tampering even without TLS (though use TLS anyway).
why_this_is_correct: Signature covers method, path, ts, body — tampering invalidates. ts in canonical string + max age check defeats replay. KeyResolver lets you rotate or revoke keys. timingSafeEqual prevents byte-leak.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A naive implementation for Client SDK signs requests with HMAC before sending; server verifies. Like AWS SigV4 simplified. only proves the demo path. It fails when inputs are empty, duplicated, unauthorized, retried, version-skewed, or observed through production telemetry because the invariant and regression signal are not explicit.

task: Throttle hook: limits how often a value updates downstream (e.g., scroll position consumers).
reasoning: Different from debounce: throttle fires at intervals during continuous activity. Useful for live UI driven by high-frequency events (scroll, resize, mousemove).
why_this_is_correct: Immediate update on first call after quiet (leading edge). Trailing edge ensures last value lands eventually. Cleanup clears pending timer on unmount.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Plain setInterval-based throttle: extra renders on idle. setTimeout per value without coalescing: stacks timers when value changes rapidly.

task: Limit worker memory in cluster mode to prevent one bad worker from consuming all RAM.
reasoning: Pass --max-old-space-size to Node to cap V8 heap per worker. Workers exiting on OOM are auto-respawned. Healthy isolation.
why_this_is_correct: Each worker capped at heap size. OOM kills the worker; primary respawns it; other workers continue serving. Optional 'restart after N requests' guards against slow leaks. Auto-respawn keeps capacity stable.
what_breaks_naive_approach: No heap cap: one worker leak takes down the whole pod. No auto-respawn: died worker = lost capacity until next deploy. Restart only on N requests but no respawn handler: capacity slowly degrades.

task: Filter change-stream events to a tenant/user — server-side pipeline so we don't ship unrelated events to consumers.
reasoning: Change streams accept an aggregation pipeline. Filter at the source rather than in the handler — saves bandwidth and CPU.
why_this_is_correct: Server-side $match: Mongo only sends matching events. fullDocument:'updateLookup' populates the full doc so the filter has tenantId to match on. Per-tenant streams scale linearly with tenant count.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Watching all changes + filtering in handler: oplog reads + network = O(total writes) per consumer. Without updateLookup, $match on fullDocument is empty for updates and you miss everything.

task: Function whose return type depends on its arguments — use overload signatures.
reasoning: When `bool ? T : U` return type is needed, overloads + an impl signature do the job. Generic conditional types can also work but overloads are more readable for fixed cases.
why_this_is_correct: Two overloads: with fallback returns Date; without returns Date|undefined. Implementation signature has the union of both. Callers see the precise return type at each call site.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Single signature `parseDate(input, fallback?): Date | undefined`: the version with fallback also returns Date|undefined, forcing a non-null assertion.

task: Detect slow clients (large send buffer for sustained time) and disconnect them to protect the server.
reasoning: Use Socket.IO documented connection behavior rather than private write-buffer internals. Coalesce the latest telemetry frame per socket, use volatile emits for lossy high-frequency data, and resume when socket.conn emits drain.
why_this_is_correct: It bounds memory to one latest frame per socket, uses volatile emits for disposable telemetry, avoids compression on the hot path, and uses the documented drain event to resume writes.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Reading private connection buffer fields couples the app to internal Engine.IO state. Plain emit at 1kHz queues stale packets for slow clients until heap growth and GC pauses take down the process.

task: Remove the X-Powered-By header to reduce fingerprinting.
reasoning: Express's X-Powered-By tells attackers what framework you're on. One line to remove. Trivial step, often overlooked.
why_this_is_correct: disable removes the header from all responses. Pair with disabling etag if you're serving APIs (etag adds CPU for no API client benefit). trust proxy 1 makes req.ip / req.protocol reflect the LB-forwarded values.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Leaving X-Powered-By: trivial fingerprint. Helmet (sec-014) handles this too — easier to just use helmet, but knowing the underlying flag matters.

task: JSON body parse errors return Express's default HTML page. Return JSON 400 instead.
reasoning: express.json() emits a SyntaxError on bad JSON. Catch in error middleware and return JSON. Distinct from validation errors (body parsed OK but fails schema).
why_this_is_correct: SyntaxError with `body` property comes from express.json. Specific check avoids catching unrelated SyntaxErrors. Mounted right after express.json so it catches parse errors before reaching routes.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Default 400 from express.json is HTML with stack trace — breaks JSON clients. Catching all SyntaxError too broadly catches programming bugs.

task: Separate API DTO from domain entity even when shapes overlap — explain when this pays off.
reasoning: Same shape today, different shape tomorrow. DTO is the API contract; entity is the persisted shape. Decoupling lets each evolve. Trivial overhead saves big refactors later.
why_this_is_correct: DTO never includes passwordHash or authVersion — internal-only. Dates serialized to strings at the boundary. Adding internal fields (e.g., lockedUntil) doesn't leak to API. Renaming entity fields doesn't break clients.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Returning entity directly: API exposes internal fields. Adding internal fields surprises API consumers. Date serialization inconsistent across endpoints.

task: Optimistic UI for chat: append message locally before ack; reconcile with server-assigned id on ack.
reasoning: Local id at send time → message appears instantly. Server returns canonical id in ack → replace local id. Track in-flight by local id so the reconcile finds the right entry.
why_this_is_correct: localId is the bridge — UI shows message immediately; ack reconciles to server id. Failed status visible to user with retry potential. Mapping by localId avoids racing other messages.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Waiting for ack before showing: laggy UX. Appending with server id only after ack: late acks cause UI to jump. No failed state: user can't retry.

task: Per-event permission filter at the broadcaster: emit only to sockets where the user has permission to see the event.
reasoning: Some events in a shared room are restricted (admin-only updates). Iterate fetchSockets and emit per-socket with permission check. Heavier than blanket broadcast but precise.
why_this_is_correct: Per-socket permission check ensures sensitive payloads never reach unauthorized clients. Parallel checks keep latency low. Returns counts for ops visibility.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Sending to all and filtering client-side: privacy violation (sensitive data on the wire). Storing per-room ACL list: drifts; users can have permissions independent of room membership.

task: Express HTTPS server requiring client certs for /internal routes only.
reasoning: Server can request client cert per-request, then verify in middleware. requestCert+rejectUnauthorized on the server allows optional client cert; middleware checks for the cert on protected routes.
why_this_is_correct: requestCert+rejectUnauthorized:false allows public routes without client cert; middleware enforces it where needed. authorized flag checks the chain validated against CA. CN allowlist scopes which client identities count.
what_breaks_naive_approach: rejectUnauthorized:true at server level: public routes break. Not checking CN: any cert signed by the CA is accepted, including for other services.

task: trust proxy misconfiguration: req.ip returns LB IP instead of client IP. Configure correctly.
reasoning: Express trust proxy controls how X-Forwarded-For is parsed. Set 1 if behind a single reverse proxy; set a CIDR if specific networks; never set true blindly (can be spoofed).
why_this_is_correct: `1` = trust last hop. CIDR allowlist = trust hops matching the specified networks. Both prevent attacker-sent X-Forwarded-For from poisoning req.ip when they connect directly.
what_breaks_naive_approach: trust proxy true: attacker sends X-Forwarded-For: 1.2.3.4 and req.ip reports 1.2.3.4, bypassing IP-based limits. Not setting trust proxy: req.ip is the LB IP, so per-IP rate limit treats all users as one IP.

task: Controlled checkbox with proper indeterminate state.
reasoning: checked is boolean; indeterminate is a separate DOM property (not an attribute), must be set via ref or effect after render.
why_this_is_correct: indeterminate set via ref after render — React doesn't manage it. checked reflects on/off; indeterminate visual overlays it. onChange resolves the ambiguity to a concrete on/off.
what_breaks_naive_approach: `indeterminate={true}` as a React prop: silently ignored (not a real attribute). Setting via getDerivedState: works but more code than the ref pattern.

task: Translate Mongoose ValidationError and duplicate-key error (E11000) into structured API responses.
reasoning: Mongoose ValidationError has .errors keyed by field. Duplicate-key error has code 11000 and keyValue. Map both to 422/409 with helpful detail.
why_this_is_correct: Each Mongo/Mongoose error type maps to a meaningful HTTP status with the right detail shape. Falls through to the generic error middleware if it's not one of these.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Generic 500 for all Mongoose errors: clients can't act (retry vs fix input). Echoing full err.message: leaks internal field names.

task: Tests reach into internal modules and break on refactor. Enforce one-way dependency direction.
reasoning: Architecture should be unidirectional: domain ← application ← interfaces (HTTP/queue). Tests should import via the same public surface. Use ESLint import-no-restricted-paths to enforce.
why_this_is_correct: ESLint enforcement at PR time blocks regressions. Domain never imports infrastructure — testing domain doesn't require infrastructure. Application orchestrates without knowing HTTP.
what_breaks_naive_approach: The naive version of Tests reach into internal modules and break on refactor. Enforce one-way dependency direction. hides the real failure mode: it may pass a demo but fails with empty data, slow services, retries, authorization boundaries, or assistive technology because the invariant is not asserted.

task: Share data between worker threads via SharedArrayBuffer to avoid copying on postMessage.
reasoning: Default Worker.postMessage copies (structured clone). For large typed arrays, transferring or SharedArrayBuffer avoids the copy. SAB allows atomic reads/writes between threads.
why_this_is_correct: SharedArrayBuffer is shared memory; worker writes are visible to main thread without postMessage copy. Atomics.store guarantees write atomicity. For large numeric crunching this avoids the structured-clone overhead.
what_breaks_naive_approach: postMessage with a big array: full structured clone, doubles memory, slow. Plain assignments without Atomics: races (rare but possible) and weak memory ordering.

task: Lazy-mount a component when it scrolls into view (e.g., heavy chart that 70% of users never see).
reasoning: react-three-fiber Canvas or a chart library lib bundle is heavy. Use IntersectionObserver to mount only when in viewport.
why_this_is_correct: IntersectionObserver fires when the sentinel enters viewport (+ rootMargin). Once visible, observer disconnects — only one mount per scroll-in. Placeholder reserves space if known.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Mount everything on load: heavy chart components blow bundle and TTI for users who scroll past. Scroll listener: jank-prone.

task: insertMany with ordered:false vs ordered:true — when each is right.
reasoning: ordered:true (default) stops at first error. ordered:false continues, reporting failures. For idempotent imports tolerating partial failure, use false. For ordered insertions (e.g., events that must be in seq), use true.
why_this_is_correct: ordered:false reports per-doc failures (e.g., duplicate keys) without aborting. ordered:true preserves order, fails on first error — appropriate for event log where holes break invariants.
what_breaks_naive_approach: ordered:true for imports: one bad row kills the whole batch — must restart from scratch. ordered:false for event logs: insertions may interleave, breaking causal order.

task: Brand primitive aliases so structurally-identical types (UserId, OrderId) don't accidentally interchange.
reasoning: Same as types-004 but focused on the why: TS is structural; without brands, all string-based ids are interchangeable. Wrong id passed compiles silently.
why_this_is_correct: Each id has a unique-symbol brand. Constructors validate prefix to enforce origin. placeOrder can't be called with placeholder strings or mixed-up id types.
what_breaks_naive_approach: The naive version of Brand primitive aliases so structurally-identical types (UserId, OrderId) don't accidentally interchange. hides the real failure mode: it may pass a demo but fails with empty data, slow services, retries, authorization boundaries, or assistive technology because the invariant is not asserted.

task: Differences between io.emit, socket.emit, socket.broadcast.emit, and socket.to(room).emit.
reasoning: For Differences between io.emit, socket.emit, socket.broadcast.emit, and socket.to(room).emit., the row-specific invariant is that Differences between io.emit, socket.emit, socket.broadcast.emit, and socket.to(room).emit. must be observable, reversible, and testable. The implementation should name the state boundary, preserve accessibility/performance constraints, and avoid hiding the failure mode behind a demo-only snippet.
why_this_is_correct: socket.* (without io.) automatically excludes sender. io.* includes everyone. .to/.in narrows by room. .except removes a room. .of selects namespace.
what_breaks_naive_approach: io.emit when you wanted socket.broadcast.emit: sender receives echo. socket.to(room).emit assumed to include sender: it doesn't.

task: Multi-service org issues JWTs from one IdP for many services. Each service must validate aud to ensure tokens aren't replayed across services.
reasoning: Same IdP, multiple audiences (aud=service-a, aud=service-b). Without aud check, a token meant for service A is accepted by service B. Pin aud in verification.
why_this_is_correct: jwt.verify with audience option enforces aud claim match. issuer check stops tokens from other IdPs. Algorithm pinning prevents algorithm confusion. Per-service verifier instances keep configuration explicit.
what_breaks_naive_approach: The naive version of Multi-service org issues JWTs from one IdP for many services. Each service must validate aud to ensure tokens aren't replayed across services. hides the real failure mode: it may pass a demo but fails with empty data, slow services, retries, authorization boundaries, or assistive technology because the invariant is not asserted.

task: Fragments with keys for lists of multi-element groups.
reasoning: Sometimes a list item renders multiple elements (e.g., dt + dd). Returning an array or Fragment lets you key both children together.
why_this_is_correct: Fragment with key groups dt+dd as a logical row without an extra DOM wrapper. Key on Fragment is honored. Alternative shorthand <> </> doesn't accept keys.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Wrapping in a <div> breaks dl semantics. Using <> </> shorthand can't carry a key. Returning arrays from .map without keys = console warning + reconciliation issues.

task: Embed trace id in error response so support can correlate to logs immediately.
reasoning: User reports an error, support asks 'what's the error code'. Include the trace id in the response body so a user can paste it. Backend logs use the same id.
why_this_is_correct: requestId in both log line and response body. Support pastes the id, finds the log line in seconds. Generic 500 message preserves privacy while id enables debugging.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Showing raw error stack to user: support can't help (user pasted html), and stack leaks internals. Logging without correlating to a user-visible id: 'I see your error but can't find which one'.

task: Lightweight contract test: ensure two services agree on a JSON shape using shared Zod schemas.
reasoning: Full pact-style contract tests are heavy. For monorepo, a shared Zod schema validated on both sides catches drift cheaply. Provider validates outgoing; consumer validates incoming.
why_this_is_correct: Same schema imported by provider and consumer. Provider validates on emit (catches drift in producer). Consumer validates on receive (catches drift in producer that escaped review). Adding a field requires updating schema, breaking both sides at compile.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Hand-typed interfaces on both sides drift silently. Validating only on consumer: provider can ship bad events. No validation: any drift = silent data corruption downstream.

task: Optimize a list of components each opening a portal — instead, share one portal with current overlay state.
reasoning: Each portal = createElement + insertion. N items = N DOM inserts. Single global overlay scales better; items just push their content.
why_this_is_correct: One portal at root. close compares id so a stale close doesn't drop someone else's overlay. Consumers don't deal with portal DOM directly.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Portal per item: each item creates and tears down a separate DOM tree. SSR check (typeof document) prevents crash on the server.

task: Unit-test a Socket.IO event handler without spinning up a full server by mocking the Socket interface.
reasoning: Full integration tests are best (sock-206) but slower. For pure handler logic, mock Socket with vi.fn. Skip transport entirely; assert the handler's emit/ack calls.
why_this_is_correct: Handler is a pure function over (socket, msg, ack). Mock Socket exposes only what handler touches. Assertions cover the emit shape, recipient room, ack value. Fast — no network, no Mongo.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Full Socket.IO server for every test: slow. Mocking everything at once produces brittle tests that pass without reflecting reality.

task: Open external links safely with rel=noopener noreferrer to prevent tabnabbing.
reasoning: Without noopener, the new tab can access window.opener and navigate the original tab. Two mitigations: noopener (security), noreferrer (privacy + security on older browsers).
why_this_is_correct: noopener stops window.opener; noreferrer also blocks Referer header. nofollow tells search engines not to pass link authority. Modern browsers now default to noopener for target=_blank, but explicit is portable.
what_breaks_naive_approach: <a target="_blank" href={...}>: in older browsers, the new tab can navigate the opener via window.opener.location. Without noreferrer, your URL leaks via Referer.

task: Sync URL query params with React state — filter values appear in the URL so links are shareable.
reasoning: State + URL must stay in sync. On state change, update URL via history.replaceState. On mount/back-button, parse URL into state. URLSearchParams + window.history.replaceState — no router needed for the basics.
why_this_is_correct: The hook now reads URL state on mount and on popstate, so Back/Forward navigation updates React state. replaceState preserves the hash fragment while keeping filter changes from flooding the history stack.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Without a popstate listener, the URL changes when the user presses Back but the UI keeps stale filters. Rebuilding the URL without location.hash silently drops in-page anchors.

task: A downstream timeout shouldn't blow up your service's p99 — bound upstream call latency relative to your request budget.
reasoning: Each request has a budget (e.g., 1s). Upstream calls get a fraction. If upstream is slow, fail fast and degrade gracefully rather than burning the whole budget.
why_this_is_correct: Budget threaded via AsyncLocalStorage. upstreamCall asks for a fraction (default half) of remaining time — preserves budget for subsequent work. AbortSignal propagates to fetch/DB calls that support it. hardCap is the absolute ceiling.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Hardcoded 30s upstream timeout: a single slow upstream consumes the whole request budget. Multiple sequential upstream calls each waiting 30s: total request time = sum of timeouts.

task: Lazy factory for an expensive singleton (DB connection) — instantiated once at first use.
reasoning: Module-level eager initialization can fail on import. Lazy factory delays until first call; subsequent calls return the cached instance.
why_this_is_correct: Inflight promise handles concurrent first-call race — both callers await the same connect. instance cached after success. Subsequent calls return synchronously.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Eager top-level await: module load throws if DB is unavailable. No inflight guard: two simultaneous first-callers each initiate connection. Mutex with bool: misses the race window.

task: React 19 useActionState for form mutations with built-in pending state.
reasoning: useActionState wraps a server action with state + pending flag. Cleaner than manual useState+useTransition for forms. Returns [state, action, isPending].
why_this_is_correct: useActionState owns pending/result state while the form remains accessible: the input has a real label, messages are associated through aria-describedby, errors use role=alert, and success uses role=status.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A visually working form without an accessible name or announced result forces screen-reader users to guess what changed. Manual loading flags also commonly get stuck after failed requests.

task: Derive a type from a const object using typeof+keyof.
reasoning: Avoid duplicating values and types. Define const, derive type from it. Same shape, single source of truth. For types-306, this matters in Derive a type from a const object using typeof+keyof: the owner boundary must be explicit, the negative fixture must fail before the fix, and state drift incidents must be observable during rollout.
why_this_is_correct: as const narrows values to literal types. keyof typeof captures keys as a union. Indexing gives the union of values. Adding to HTTP_STATUS automatically updates both name and code unions.
what_breaks_naive_approach: The naive version of Derive a type from a const object using typeof+keyof. hides the real failure mode: it may pass a demo but fails with empty data, slow services, retries, authorization boundaries, or assistive technology because the invariant is not asserted.

task: Force websocket-only transport (skip polling fallback) when you control both client and server.
reasoning: Polling exists for compat. If clients are modern + you don't need legacy support, websocket-only is faster and simpler (no sticky session needed for handshake).
why_this_is_correct: transports:['websocket'] alone = no polling fallback. allowUpgrades:false explicitly turns off upgrade negotiation. Server and client must match. No sticky session needed because there's no polling handshake.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Mixed transports default lets polling clients in but requires sticky sessions across load balancer — operational complexity for legacy support most apps don't need.

task: Internal service hardcodes the public key of an issuer instead of fetching JWKS — defeats compromised IdP.
reasoning: JWKS fetching is the standard. But for internal services in tight environments, pinning the public key directly removes the JWKS endpoint as an attack surface. Trade-off: manual rotation.
why_this_is_correct: Public key embedded in the service's config — no network call to verify. Compromised IdP can't issue tokens accepted by your service without also stealing the private key. Rotation = redeploy with new public key (operational cost, but explicit).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Fetching JWKS on each verify: rate-limited eventually, latency per request, network single-point-of-failure. Caching JWKS forever: rotation invisible. Pinning suits internal services with infrequent rotation.

task: Component derives state from prop on first render but allows local override. Common bug: state stays stale when prop changes.
reasoning: Pattern: initialize useState from prop. Prop changes don't update state — useEffect to sync, OR use key prop to remount. Pick by intent: 'reset on key change' is the cleanest pattern.
why_this_is_correct: Local edits are preserved unless the explicit resetKey changes. The optional key-prop pattern remains available when the parent wants a full remount.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Syncing whenever initialValue changes erases unsaved user edits. Never syncing leaves the editor stuck on the first entity when the parent changes records.

task: Return all validation errors at once (instead of first) so users fix the form in one pass.
reasoning: Zod by default collects all errors. flatten().fieldErrors gives per-field arrays. Return all in 422 response; UI shows next to each field.
why_this_is_correct: Zod's .safeParse collects all errors. flatten().fieldErrors is { email: ['Invalid email'], password: ['Too short', 'Missing uppercase'] } — perfect for forms to render per-field. One round trip per submit attempt.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Returning first error only: user fixes field A, submits, sees field B error, etc. — terrible UX. Custom validators that throw on first failure leak the same anti-pattern.

task: DTOs use ISO strings for dates; entities use Date. Centralize the conversion at the boundary.
reasoning: JSON has no native Date — must serialize. Choosing ISO 8601 strings is the standard. Centralize so all endpoints serialize the same way (UTC, with timezone designator).
why_this_is_correct: Always UTC, always ISO 8601. Single helper functions used by all mappers. Validation in isoToDate rejects malformed strings. Nullable helpers avoid scattered ternaries.
what_breaks_naive_approach: `new Date(value)` in handlers without UTC awareness produces local-time strings. Sending entity directly: Date.toJSON() gives ISO, but clients can't deserialize back to Date typed; manual mapping makes the round-trip explicit.

task: Tailwind v4 JIT mode generates only used classes — verify the build is tree-shaking properly.
reasoning: Tailwind v4 is content-driven: scans your files, emits only used classes. Dynamic class names (cn(`bg-${color}`)) bypass scanning. Use safelist or static maps.
why_this_is_correct: Static maps make class names visible to Tailwind's scanner. safelist is the escape hatch for truly dynamic patterns. Dynamic concatenation `bg-${color}-500` is invisible to the scanner — those classes won't be emitted.
what_breaks_naive_approach: `className={`bg-${dynamicColor}-500`}`: Tailwind doesn't see these in source, doesn't emit them, classes don't apply at runtime. safelist is a sledgehammer that defeats tree-shaking — use sparingly.

task: Add a fast-fail timeout to a fetch call using Promise.race against a setTimeout.
reasoning: AbortController is the right answer for fetch (cancels the actual request). Promise.race version is simpler but the underlying request continues. Use AbortController when possible.
why_this_is_correct: AbortController actually cancels — the network request stops, freeing the socket. TimeoutError vs other errors distinguishable. clearTimeout in finally prevents the timer firing after success.
what_breaks_naive_approach: The naive version of Add a fast-fail timeout to a fetch call using Promise.race against a setTimeout. hides the real failure mode: it may pass a demo but fails with empty data, slow services, retries, authorization boundaries, or assistive technology because the invariant is not asserted.

task: AtLeastOne<T> type that requires at least one of T's keys to be present.
reasoning: Useful for update DTOs where empty patch is meaningless. Compose by mapping over keys and OR-ing required-one versions.
why_this_is_correct: Mapped type produces a union where each member requires one specific key. Intersecting with Partial<T> keeps the others optional. Calling with empty object fails because none of the union members match.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Partial<T> alone allows empty objects. Runtime check throws at request time — type-level enforcement catches it at compile.

task: Detect Redis adapter health: if pub/sub Redis is down, mark instance unhealthy so LB pulls it.
reasoning: Redis adapter publishes cross-instance emits. If Redis is unreachable, those emits go nowhere — instance is operating in isolation. Detect via ping; degrade readiness probe.
why_this_is_correct: ioredis emits 'end'/'error'/'ready' as connection state changes. Flag tracks last known state cheaply. deepCheck verifies via ping for readiness probe. Combine with conditional 503 in /readiness endpoint (perf-301).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Trusting connection state at boot: Redis can fail mid-life with no signal. Pinging on every request: latency overhead. Polling without event subscription: late detection.

task: Restrict allowed HTTP methods globally; reject TRACE/CONNECT.
reasoning: TRACE can echo headers (incl. cookies) back — XST attack vector. CONNECT used for proxying. Most apps need only GET/POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE/OPTIONS/HEAD.
why_this_is_correct: Set lookup is O(1). 405 with Allow header is correct per RFC. Rejects exotic methods early. Run before routes.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Allowing TRACE: XST exfiltrates cookies in some configurations. Letting unknown methods reach routes: Express returns 404 with HTML.

task: Read store value imperatively (e.g., inside an event handler) without subscribing to re-renders.
reasoning: useSelector subscribes — re-renders on change. For one-off reads (event handler closure that doesn't need to react), use a ref or direct store.get(). Avoid the cost.
why_this_is_correct: getSnapshot is stable across renders; calling it reads fresh state without subscribing. Event handler sees latest value when invoked. Component doesn't re-render on count change.
what_breaks_naive_approach: useSelector for state you don't render: unnecessary re-renders on every change. Saving state in a useEffect-managed ref: complex and error-prone.

task: Map known external API errors (Stripe-specific) into your typed error hierarchy so handlers stay clean.
reasoning: External SDK errors leak provider-specific types. Wrap at the boundary; throw domain errors. Provides a single translation point and isolates handlers from SDK changes.
why_this_is_correct: Stripe-specific types stay isolated. Handlers catch AppError subclasses. Mapping table makes it obvious what each provider error means to the business. Swapping providers = swap mapper.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Catching raw Stripe errors in handlers: provider-specific types leak into business logic, hard to swap or test.

task: Some deps should be per-request (e.g., transaction-scoped repo). Build a request-scoped container.
reasoning: Singletons don't fit transactions. Per-request container creates fresh scoped instances; cleaned up at request end. Combine with DI container (arch-002).
why_this_is_correct: Lazy resolution + per-instance cache (one resolution per request). Cleanup on response end disposes transaction-scoped resources. Handler grabs deps via req.scope.resolve.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Reusing a singleton transaction across requests: cross-request data corruption. Manual instantiation in each handler: forgotten cleanup leaks DB sessions.

task: Use React 19 prefetchDNS and preconnect APIs for resource hints declaratively.
reasoning: React 19 adds `prefetchDNS`, `preconnect`, `preinit` APIs that emit resource hints into the document head. Cleaner than manually managing <link> elements.
why_this_is_correct: React 19 functions queue resource hints during render — React inserts the right <link> tags. Order-independent because React dedupes and prioritizes. Idiomatic React, no manual DOM management.
what_breaks_naive_approach: useEffect inserting <link> elements: runs after first paint, missing the preconnect window. Inline <link rel=...> in JSX: hard to compute dynamically based on data.

task: Detect at compile time that a switch covers all branches using a helper that maps to never.
reasoning: Same family as types-101 / types-203. This example shows the negative case explicitly — what happens when a new branch is added.
why_this_is_correct: Compiler enforces exhaustiveness via the `never` assignment. New branch added to Mode = compile error in default branch — pinpoints every dispatch site.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Default returning a string: silent fallthrough for new branches. Hand-written 'unreachable' Error without never check: only triggers at runtime if reached.

task: Admin tool to broadcast to specific users by id, even across cluster instances.
reasoning: Pair with per-user room (sock-112) and Redis/cluster adapter. io.to(`user:${id}`).emit reaches all of user's sockets across all instances.
why_this_is_correct: io.to() accepts an array of rooms — single emit reaches all listed rooms' members. With Redis adapter, this fans out across the cluster. disconnectSockets(true) closes connections cluster-wide.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Iterating fetchSockets and emitting per user: doesn't span cluster, only this instance's sockets. Manually deleting Redis adapter state: corrupts subsequent emits.

task: Reject weak passwords using a known-leaked password list (via SHA-1 prefix) — k-anonymity API like HIBP.
reasoning: HaveIBeenPwned exposes prefix-based lookup: send first 5 hex chars of SHA1; receive list of suffixes + counts. Compare locally. Doesn't leak full hash to the service.
why_this_is_correct: k-anonymity: HIBP sees the 5-char prefix; suffix never leaves your server. Add-Padding mitigates traffic-analysis. Sha1 acceptable here (anonymity-preserving identifier, not security).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Sending the full password (or hash) to a third party: exposes user credentials. Local list lookup: 800M passwords = unmanageable on app servers without specialized infra.

task: Controlled numeric input that handles empty + invalid intermediate states cleanly.
reasoning: `type="number"` value as number forces empty input to '' which violates 'must be number'. Pattern: store value as string in state, parse on submit. OR debounce parse with NaN handling.
why_this_is_correct: Storing text preserves intermediate states (empty, '-', '1.'). onChange reports parsed number or null. inputMode='numeric' triggers numeric keyboard on mobile while keeping freeform input behavior.
what_breaks_naive_approach: `type=number` value={value}: typing '.' or '-' becomes NaN, value invalid → '' shown → controlled-uncontrolled warning. Parsing too eagerly turns '1.' into 1 and re-renders to '1', cursor jumps.

task: Avoid returning 200 with `{ error: ... }` body. Use proper HTTP status codes so middleware/observability works.
reasoning: 200 with error body breaks every middleware that expects status to indicate outcome — metrics, retries, caching. Status codes are the contract.
why_this_is_correct: 4xx for client errors. 5xx for server errors. 200 only for success. APM tools track error rate from status; alerting works. Frontend HTTP libraries treat non-2xx as errors automatically.
what_breaks_naive_approach: 200 with error body: APM error rate is 0 even during outage. fetch() doesn't throw on 200, so frontend must inspect every body field. Retries based on status (e.g., 503) never trigger.
